q: The compiler allows bad input. Pick the fix.

opts.t: Keep any, it works at runtime
opts.why: `any` disables all type-checking — a number or null will throw at runtime.

opts.t: Change the parameter type to unknown
Add a return type annotation
opts.why: A return type won't help — the unsafe access is on the parameter.

why: `unknown` is the type-safe top type: you can assign anything to it, but you cannot use it until you narrow. With `typeof x === 'string'` before `.toUpperCase()`, the compiler guarantees the call is safe. `any` silently skips that check.
fj: "Just use any — TypeScript is too strict anyway."
fs: "Use unknown and narrow with typeof so the compiler proves safety before you call methods."

q: What is the type of `palette` after this line?

opts.t: string[]
opts.why: Without `as const`, TS widens to `string[]`. The `as const` changes that.

opts.t: readonly [string, string, string]
opts.why: Closer, but the element type is still widened — each slot would be `string`, not the literal.

opts.t: readonly ['red', 'green', 'blue']

why: `as const` freezes the inferred type to a `readonly` tuple of literal types. Without it, TS widens `'red'` to `string`. The literal tuple lets you use `typeof palette[number]` as a union `'red' | 'green' | 'blue'` for exhaustive checks.
fj: "as const is cosmetic — the type is still string[]"
fs: "as const locks string literals in place; you get a readonly literal tuple, not string[]."

q: Narrowing the union fails at the `if`. Pick the fix.

opts.t: Add a type assertion: s as { kind: 'circle'; r: number }
opts.why: An assertion lies to the compiler and bypasses the real check.

opts.t: Change s.type to s.kind
Use typeof s === 'circle'
opts.why: typeof returns object kinds like 'object', not discriminant values.

why: The discriminant property is `kind`, not `type`. TS uses discriminated unions: when every member has a common literal-typed field, narrowing on that field reduces the union to a single branch. Checking the wrong property name (`s.type`) gives a compile error because `type` doesn't exist on the union.
fj: "The type is fine — just add a cast."
fs: "Always narrow on the actual discriminant field; using the wrong key is a compile-time error, which is the point."

q: The generic function rejects a valid call. Pick the fix.

opts.t: Constrain T with extends { toString(): string }
opts.why: The return type is T, not string — calling .toString() and returning it is a type mismatch.

opts.t: Return arr[0] directly — remove the .toString() call
Change the return type to string
opts.why: Changing the return type to string defeats the purpose of the generic and narrows callers unexpectedly.

why: The function promises to return `T`, but `.toString()` produces a `string`, which is not assignable to `T`. The correct fix is to return `arr[0]` — the conversion was unnecessary and broke the generic contract. A generic should preserve the type, not coerce it.
fj: "Generics are just any under the hood — it works at runtime."
fs: "A generic function must return the declared type T; coercing with .toString() breaks the contract."

q: You want to validate shape while keeping the type. Pick the idiomatic version.

opts.t: Option A — annotate with : Config
opts.why: A type annotation widens the inferred type to `Config`, losing the literal `5432` and `'db'`.

opts.t: Both are identical in all respects
opts.why: They differ: annotation widens; satisfies validates without widening.

opts.t: Option B — use satisfies Config

why: `satisfies` (TS 4.9+) checks that the value matches the type but keeps the inferred literal type. With `: Config` annotation the type of `cfg.port` is `number`; with `satisfies Config` it stays `5432`. Use `satisfies` when you want validation but need the narrow type for autocomplete or exhaustive checks.
fj: "satisfies and : Type are the same thing — pick whichever looks nicer."
fs: "satisfies validates without widening; a type annotation widens the inferred type to the declared one."

q: This compiles but has a hidden runtime bug. What is it?

opts.t: Record<string, string> is the wrong type for a user map
opts.why: The type itself is valid; the problem is how the result is used.

opts.t: The non-null assertion ! hides a real undefined
toUpperCase() does not exist on string
opts.why: toUpperCase() is a valid string method — that is not the problem.

why: `users[id]` returns `string | undefined` when `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` is on, or `string` with the index signature but can still be `undefined` at runtime when the key is missing. The `!` operator tells the compiler "trust me, not null" — it compiles away entirely. If the key is absent, `.toUpperCase()` throws a TypeError. Check for undefined before using the value.
fj: "The ! is just to satisfy the compiler — it's safe to use."
fs: "Non-null assertion ! is a runtime lie; always guard with an undefined check instead."

q: You need UserId and OrderId to be incompatible strings. Pick the idiomatic fix.
lines: // Bug: these are now the same type

opts.t: Wrap each in a class with a private field
opts.why: Classes add runtime overhead and change the usage pattern entirely — overkill for a scalar ID.

opts.t: Use a branded type: type UserId = string & { readonly _brand: 'UserId' }
Change both types to number
opts.why: Changing the base type does not fix the structural equivalence — both would still be `number` and interchangeable.

why: TypeScript uses structural typing, so two `string` aliases are identical. A branded type intersects the primitive with a phantom readonly tag (`{ readonly _brand: 'UserId' }`). The tag never exists at runtime but makes the types nominally distinct at compile time. You provide a single factory/assertion function to create values, so callers cannot pass a raw string accidentally.
fj: "Type aliases are fine — just document which ID is which."
fs: "Brand your IDs with a phantom intersection so the compiler enforces correct usage across call sites."

q: The function silently mutates the caller's array. Pick the fix.

opts.t: Cast names to string[] with (names as string[]).sort()
opts.why: A cast lets you call sort() but still mutates the original array — the readonly contract is violated.

opts.t: Copy first: return [...names].sort()
Change the parameter to string[] so sort() is allowed
opts.why: Removing readonly broadens the API contract and still allows mutation — the wrong fix.

why: `readonly string[]` (or `ReadonlyArray<string>`) blocks mutation methods like `sort()`, `push()`, and `splice()` at compile time. `Array.prototype.sort` sorts in-place, so calling it on the array the caller passed would mutate their data. The correct fix is to spread into a new array (`[...names]`) before sorting, so the original is untouched.
fj: "Just cast it — readonly is a hint, not enforced at runtime."
fs: "Spread into a new array before sorting; never cast away readonly to satisfy the compiler."

q: This crashes at runtime even though it compiles. What is wrong?

opts.t: The return type should be string | undefined
opts.why: Changing the return type hides the bug — the crash still happens on the ! assertion.

opts.t: The non-null assertion ! will throw when address is absent
Optional fields cannot be nested; use a union instead
opts.why: Nested optional fields are perfectly valid TypeScript — that is not the issue.

why: `u.address` is optional, so it can be `undefined`. The `!` non-null assertion tells the compiler "I promise this is defined" — it compiles away to nothing. When `address` really is `undefined`, accessing `.city` on it throws `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined`. Use optional chaining `u.address?.city` and let the return type be `string | undefined`, or guard with `if (u.address)` before accessing.
fj: "The ! is just to keep the compiler happy — it's safe."
fs: "Optional chaining u.address?.city is safe; ! is a lie that crashes when the value is absent."

q: What does this enum print at runtime? Pick the correct answer.

opts.t: "Up" then undefined — reverse lookup only works for string enums
opts.why: Numeric enums compile to an object with both forward and reverse mappings.

opts.t: 0 then "Up" — numeric enums have reverse mappings
"Up" then "Up" — TypeScript rewrites both to the name
opts.why: TypeScript does not rewrite member accesses to names; Direction.Up compiles to the numeric value 0.

opts.t: 0 then 0 — enums are just numbers
opts.why: Direction[0] uses the reverse mapping to look up 'Up', not the numeric value again.

why: Numeric enums (no explicit string values) compile to a JS object with both a forward mapping (`Direction.Up === 0`) and a reverse mapping (`Direction[0] === 'Up'`). This is a runtime surprise: `Object.keys(Direction)` returns both names and number strings. String enums do NOT get a reverse mapping, so `Direction[0]` would be `undefined` for them. When you need safe iteration, prefer `const` objects or string enums.
fj: "Enums are just numbers — Direction.Up is 0 and that's all."
fs: "Numeric enums compile a two-way mapping; string enums don't — know which you're using."

q: The narrowing works inside the function but is lost in the callback. Why?

opts.t: setTimeout callbacks are not type-checked
opts.why: setTimeout callbacks are fully type-checked — narrowing is the issue, not callback support.

opts.t: Narrowing does not flow into callbacks; capture val in a const
The null guard must be repeated inside the callback
opts.why: Repeating the guard works around the problem but the real fix is understanding why narrowing doesn't persist.

why: TypeScript's control-flow narrowing only holds at the point of the check. When you create a closure (like a `setTimeout` callback), TS conservatively assumes the variable could be reassigned before the callback runs — so it reverts the type to `string | null`. The idiomatic fix is `const safeVal = val;` right after the guard: the `const` binding can never be reassigned, so TS confidently narrows `safeVal` to `string` inside the closure.
fj: "The narrowing is global — once I check, TS knows it everywhere."
fs: "Assign the narrowed value to a const before creating a closure; closures reset narrowing on mutable bindings."

q: The switch is missing a branch but TS doesn't error. What is the fix?

opts.t: Add a default that throws — TS then errors on unhandled members
opts.why: A plain throw in default doesn't make TS error at compile time for missing cases.

opts.t: Add a default with an exhaustiveness check using never
Enable strictNullChecks — that flag catches missing cases
opts.why: strictNullChecks is about null/undefined; it does not enforce exhaustiveness in switch statements.

why: TypeScript doesn't error on a non-exhaustive switch by default because the function can return `undefined` implicitly. The idiomatic fix is an exhaustiveness check in the `default` branch: `default: const _: never = e; throw new Error('Unhandled event');`. After the `click` and `keydown` cases, `e` is narrowed to the `scroll` variant. Assigning it to `never` causes a compile error — `scroll` is not assignable to `never` — which forces you to add the missing case.
fj: "No compiler error means the switch is fine — TypeScript would tell me if I missed something."
fs: "Add a default: const _: never = e branch; assigning the leftover union to never makes the compiler error on missing cases."

q: This double assertion compiles and ships. What is the prod risk?
lines: return raw as unknown as Config;

opts.t: fetch().then(r => r.json()) should use r.text()
opts.why: r.json() is correct for JSON APIs; the issue is the double assertion, not the fetch method.

opts.t: The double assertion hides schema drift: API can change without a type error
Promise<Config> must be awaited before asserting
opts.why: The await is already applied via .then(); the Promise is resolved before the assertion.

opts.t: as unknown as T is a compile error in strict mode
opts.why: as unknown as T is always legal — it is the intentional escape hatch, not a compiler error.

why: `as unknown as Config` is the universal override: it silences every type mismatch by routing through `unknown`. At the point it's written it looks harmless. In production, when the API contract changes — a field renamed, a number returned as a string, a nested object gone missing — no TS error surfaces. The fix is runtime validation with a schema library (Zod, Valibot, io-ts): `Config.parse(raw)` throws at the boundary if the shape drifts, turning a silent runtime corruption into an immediate loud failure.
fj: "The compiler accepts it — the types match."
fs: "Parse at the API boundary with Zod; as unknown as T suppresses every future schema mismatch silently."

q: What prod bug does this generic inference introduce?

opts.t: wrap([]) should be wrap<never[]>([]) to fix inference
opts.why: Explicitly passing never[] makes the type worse — you'd still be stuck with never[].

opts.t: T pins to never[] for empty arrays; pushing to c.value fails at compile time later
wrap cannot infer array types; use an overload
opts.why: wrap infers array types correctly when elements are present; the issue is only with empty arrays.

why: When TypeScript infers `T` from `[]`, it has no information about element type and falls back to `never[]`. Anything you then try to push into `c.value` is a compile error because `never` has no inhabitants. This is a common subtle bug in generic utilities: a caller passes an empty array expecting to fill it later, but the type is permanently `never[]`. Fix with a default: `function wrap<T = unknown>(val: T)` or require callers to annotate: `wrap<number[]>([])`. In library code, document this limitation.
fj: "Empty arrays are just arrays — TS figures it out when you add elements."
fs: "TS pins empty-array T to never[]; annotate explicitly wrap<number[]>([]) or provide a default type parameter."

q: A colleague adds an interface in a separate file. What runtime surprise appears?

opts.t: The two interfaces merge silently; a real Express Request lacks log()
TypeScript errors on duplicate interface names across files
opts.why: Declaration merging is intentional in TypeScript — it does NOT error on duplicate interface names.

opts.t: The express.d.ts interface wins; logger.ts interface is ignored
opts.why: TypeScript merges all declarations of the same interface name; neither wins over the other.

why: TypeScript's *declaration merging* silently combines all `interface` declarations with the same name in the same scope. Here `Request` in `logger.ts` merges with Express's global `Request`, so the compiler believes every `Request` has a `log()` method. At runtime, real Express request objects have no such method — calling `req.log()` throws `TypeError`. Always namespace augmentations (`interface MyRequest extends Request { log(): void }`) and avoid naming local interfaces the same as well-known globals.
fj: "TypeScript would error if two files declare the same interface."
fs: "Declaration merging silently combines same-name interfaces; name your local extensions to avoid colliding with globals."

q: This compiles. Is the callback parameter type safe?

opts.t: Yes — widening a callback parameter is always safe
opts.why: Widening is safe in this direction, but the issue is that method shorthand enables bivariance which can also go the other way.

opts.t: Method shorthand enables bivariance; a narrower callback would compile but crash
The conditional typeof check makes x: number | string unsafe
opts.why: The typeof guard is fine; the type-safety concern is about bivariant method signatures, not the guard.

why: TypeScript method shorthand (`onData(x: string): void`) is *bivariant* for historical compatibility: both widening AND narrowing the parameter type is accepted. This means `{ onData(x: number): void }` would also satisfy `Handler`, even though the caller always passes strings — the number-only handler would receive a string and could fail silently. Using function property syntax (`onData: (x: string) => void`) enables strict function-type checking (contravariant parameters) and catches the unsafe narrowing. Enable `strictFunctionTypes` to make this the default.
fj: "Methods that accept more types than declared are always safe."
fs: "Method shorthand is bivariant; use property-arrow syntax + strictFunctionTypes to catch unsound parameter narrowing."

q: A library ships this change. CI stays green. Which consumers break silently?

opts.t: No consumers break — adding a union member is always backward compatible
opts.why: Adding a member is a breaking change for any consumer with exhaustive checks on the role field.

opts.t: Consumers with exhaustive switch or never-checks on role silently miss 'editor'
CI breaks because User is re-exported and callers recompile
opts.why: If consumers pin to a published version, they won't recompile against v2 until they upgrade — the break is deferred, not caught by CI.

opts.t: Only runtime breaks matter; type changes don't affect shipped code
opts.why: Type changes affect consumer code at compile time when they upgrade; the break is real.

why: Expanding a discriminated union is a **breaking type change** even though it looks additive. Any consumer that switches on `role` with a `default: const _: never = role` exhaustiveness check will now get a compile error when they upgrade — `'editor'` is not assignable to `never`. Worse, consumers who use `if (role === 'admin') ... else // viewer` silently route `'editor'` down the wrong branch. Treat union expansions as breaking changes in your semver policy, document them in changelogs, and give consumers a migration window. Consider a `@deprecated` overlay type.
fj: "Adding a value to a union is non-breaking — we're only adding, not removing."
fs: "Union expansion is a breaking change for exhaustive-check consumers; semver-major it and document the migration."

q: This conditional type ships in a public API. What is the org-level risk?

opts.t: Recursive conditional types are a compiler bug; report to Microsoft
opts.why: Recursive conditional types are supported; the issue is an authorship problem, not a TS bug.

opts.t: Overly deep recursion produces unreadable errors; consumers can't diagnose failures
The type is incorrect; Flatten should use mapped types instead
opts.why: Mapped types solve a different problem; conditional recursion for flattening is legitimate — depth and error clarity are the concerns.

why: Recursive conditional types are powerful but can exceed TypeScript's recursion depth limit (typically ~100 levels), producing the cryptic 'Type instantiation is excessively deep' error at consumer call sites — not inside your library. The consumer sees only the effect, not the cause. At org scale this means: support tickets, blocked builds, people adding `// @ts-ignore` hacks. Mitigation: cap recursion with a depth counter tuple `[...Acc, 0]`, provide simpler overloads for common depths, and add error messages via `never & { error: 'Too deeply nested' }` in the base case.
fj: "Recursive types are fine if they compile in our own codebase."
fs: "Cap recursion depth and emit readable error messages in the never branch — your consumers see the errors, not you."

q: This public API returns an internal mutable type. What is the blast radius?

opts.t: Callers can mutate the returned array, corrupting the store
Map.get returns a copy, so the store is safe
opts.why: Map.get returns a reference to the stored value — objects and arrays are not copied.

opts.t: The return type should be Promise<User[]> for async correctness
opts.why: Synchronous map access is fine; the bug is about mutability, not async patterns.

why: JavaScript Maps store references. `store.get(tenantId)` returns the exact same `User[]` instance that lives in the store. Any caller who does `getUsers('t1').push(fakeUser)` silently mutates the internal store — now every future call to `getUsers('t1')` returns polluted data. At org scale with multiple teams consuming the SDK, this is an invisible cross-tenant data hazard. Fix: return `ReadonlyArray<Readonly<User>>` in the type and `[...users]` or `Object.freeze(users)` at runtime. Prefer immutable data structures (Immer, `as const` objects) for shared state.
fj: "Callers won't mutate the array — it's their responsibility not to."
fs: "Return ReadonlyArray and a spread copy; callers WILL push to your array, so make mutation a compile error."

q: A Zod schema and its inferred type diverge. Which scenario is the risk?

opts.t: Zod and TypeScript types are always in sync — infer keeps them identical
opts.why: z.infer<typeof UserSchema> stays in sync automatically, but only if that derived type is actually used — a parallel handwritten type can drift.

opts.t: Handwritten type adds 'editor' and email; validated data silently carries wrong shape downstream
The schema is the source of truth; the handwritten type is ignored at runtime
opts.why: TypeScript types are erased at runtime; the handwritten type shapes compile-time code that processes validated data incorrectly.

why: The Zod schema is the runtime contract; `z.infer<typeof UserSchema>` derives the TypeScript type from it automatically and can never drift. A separately maintained `User` type in `types.ts` can grow extra members (`'editor'`, `email?`) that the schema will never produce. Code that accepts `User` and expects `email` will read `undefined` silently; code that exhaustively handles `'editor'` will never trigger but mask the gap. **Golden rule**: derive, don't duplicate — export `export type User = z.infer<typeof UserSchema>` and delete the handwritten copy. Any schema change immediately propagates to all consumers.
fj: "We keep the type in types.ts so it's easy to find and edit."
fs: "Always derive the TS type from the Zod schema with z.infer; a parallel handwritten type drifts silently and corrupts downstream logic."
